title: Reading and Use of English – Part 1
description: Read the text below and decide which answer (A, B, C or D) best fits each gap.

content.text: Deep beneath the ocean's surface, where sunlight (0) penetrates, a remarkable phenomenon known as bioluminescence takes place. This natural ability of organisms to (1) light is not just a visual spectacle but a vital survival strategy. In the (2) darkness of the midnight zone, creatures have evolved various ways to utilize this glowing energy. For some, it serves to (3) prey, acting as a lure that mimics smaller, edible organisms. Others use light as a (4) of communication, signaling to potential mates across the vast, empty expanses of the sea. Interestingly, certain species use "counter-illumination" to (5) themselves from predators lurking below. By matching the faint light filtering from above, they effectively (6) into the background. While land-based bioluminescence is relatively rare, it is the (7) rather than the exception in the deep ocean. Scientists are still working to (8) the mysteries of how these chemical reactions are triggered so efficiently. As technology improves, we are gaining a better understanding of how these radiant displays have shaped the evolution of marine life over millions of years, proving that life finds a way to shine even in the harshest and most inhospitable environments on our planet, and it remains truly fascinating.

example.explanation: “Barely” is the most common adverb used to describe the minimal penetration of light in physical contexts. While the others are synonyms, “barely penetrates” is the standard collocation here.

questions.explanation: “Emit light” is the precise scientific collocation for organisms producing light naturally. “Radiate” usually implies heat, and “discharge” is used for waste or electricity.
“Absolute darkness” is a fixed phrase describing a complete lack of light. “Sheer” is typically used for size or intensity (e.g., sheer luck), and “utter” for sounds or abstract concepts.
“Entice prey” is the correct collocation in biological contexts meaning to attract through temptation. “Charm” and “appeal” are too human-centric.
“A means of communication” is a fixed idiomatic phrase. While “method” or “way” might seem plausible, “means” is the standard academic term for this function.
“Conceal themselves” is the standard collocation for camouflage. “Shield” implies protection from a physical blow, and “veil” is used for obscuring sight with a fabric.
“Blend into the background” is a fixed phrasal verb/idiom used for camouflage. The others do not fit the context of visual integration.
“The rule rather than the exception” is a fixed idiomatic expression meaning something is common rather than rare.
“Unravel the mysteries” is a very common figurative collocation. “Unlock” is often used for secrets, but “unravel” fits the complex nature of scientific discovery.
